answers.field_label: Describe How The Injury Illness Happened
answers.value:
On the morning of 2026-01-15, I was working my regular shift as a custodial worker at Edison High School, part of the Fresno Unified School District Maintenance & Operations Department. At approximately 10:35 a.m., I was in the main custodial storage room located near the east wing hallway, restocking cleaning supplies and paper products for the classrooms and restrooms.

Earlier that morning, a delivery of bulk supplies had been dropped off and stacked on a pallet just inside the storage room. The pallet contained multiple large boxes of copy paper, paper towels, toilet paper, and cleaning solution. I had already moved a few lighter boxes without any issues. The incident occurred when I attempted to move a heavier, awkwardly sized box containing multiple gallon jugs of floor cleaner and disinfectant (roughly 40–50 pounds based on the label and my experience handling these boxes).

Because the storage room is fairly narrow and there were other boxes stacked around the pallet, I did not have a lot of space to position myself directly in front of the box. I ended up standing slightly to the side of it. I bent at the waist more than I should have instead of squatting fully with my knees, and I grabbed the box with both hands, with most of the weight on my right side. I then tried to lift and pivot in one motion so I could place the box onto a metal shelf that is about waist height.

As I lifted and twisted to my left at the same time, I immediately felt a sharp, pulling pain in my lower back, slightly to the right of my spine, followed by a tight, cramping sensation that made it difficult to straighten up. At the same moment, I also felt a sudden, stabbing pain in my right shoulder and a jolt of pain in my right wrist, like I had overextended or strained it while gripping the box. The pain caused me to lose my balance slightly and I had to set the box down quickly and somewhat awkwardly on the floor to avoid dropping it.

I paused for a moment, leaning against the shelf, trying to catch my breath. I noticed that when I tried to stand completely upright, there was significant stiffness and a strong, pulling pain in my lower back, and my right shoulder felt weak and sore, especially when I tried to raise my arm above shoulder level. My right wrist felt tender and slightly shaky when I flexed it. I had not felt any of these pains before that lift; they started immediately with that specific movement.

Within a few minutes, the pain in my lower back intensified and began to radiate slightly down into my right hip area. I realized this was more than just minor soreness, so I stopped what I was doing. I walked slowly to the main office and informed my supervisor, Michael Thompson, of what had happened. I explained that I had been lifting a heavy box of cleaning supplies from a pallet in the storage room, that the space was tight, and that I had twisted while lifting. I told him I felt a sharp pain in my lower back and discomfort in my right shoulder and wrist.

My supervisor asked me to sit down and rest for a few minutes and observed that I was moving stiffly and cautiously. After I tried to stand up again and still had significant pain and limited range of motion, he recommended that I report the injury and seek medical evaluation through the district’s workers’ compensation process. He accompanied me to the office where I could sit while arrangements were made for me to be seen at the occupational health clinic.

To summarize, the injury occurred on the employer’s premises during my normal work shift, while I was performing my regular job duties of moving and stocking custodial supplies. The primary contributing factors were the weight of the box, the awkward position and limited space around the pallet, and the fact that I lifted and twisted at the same time instead of repositioning myself or asking for assistance. There were no wet floors, no equipment malfunctions, and no involvement of any other person; it was not an assault or intentional act by anyone. It was a strain/sprain type injury caused by improper body mechanics and handling a heavy load under less-than-ideal space conditions. I had not taken any medication, alcohol, or drugs, and I was not distracted by a phone or anything else at the time of the incident. I was focused on finishing the restocking task before the next class period change when the hallways get busy, which probably made me rush the lift instead of taking the extra time to move the pallet or ask for help.
